rows.v: The best possible hand in standard poker: A-K-Q-J-10 all of the same suit. It cannot be beaten. The odds of being dealt one in five cards are about 1 in 649,740.
After three hours of folding, the river completed his royal flush. The table went silent, then erupted — the dealer admitted she'd never seen one in ten years on the floor.

A 20th-century American term highlighting the 'royalty' of the court cards topping the hand. It's technically the highest straight flush, and in video poker it's the jackpot, typically paying 4,000-to-1.
Five cards in sequence, all the same suit (for example 6-7-8-9-10 of hearts). The second-strongest hand, beaten only by a higher straight flush or a royal.
He flopped a straight flush and slow-played it perfectly, letting his opponent bet into a hand that was already unbeatable.

The name simply combines a 'straight' (a run of cards) and a 'flush' (one suit). Both terms predate poker, drawn from older card games and, in 'flush', ultimately from the idea of cards 'flowing' together in one suit.
Four cards of the same rank, such as four kings, plus any fifth card. Also called 'quads.' A very strong and rare hand.
'I've got quads,' she said flatly, flipping over four nines. It's one of the few hands you can announce without fear of a bluff.
'Of a kind' meaning 'of the same type' dates to the 16th century. The shorthand 'quads' is modern poker-room slang, mirroring 'trips' for three of a kind.
Three cards of one rank plus two of another (for example three queens and two sevens). Ranks below four of a kind but above a flush.
He filled up on the turn — pocket pairs becoming a full house — and suddenly his cautious early checks looked like a trap.
Originally called a 'full hand' in 19th-century play; 'full house' became standard by the early 1900s. The image is of a hand that is 'full' — every card contributing to the combination.
The best possible hand given the cards on the table, which by definition cannot be beaten at that moment.
'I have the nuts here,' he announced, knowing no combination of remaining cards could top his straight. The only question was how much he could extract.
The term likely comes from old gambling: a player would place the nuts of his wagon wheels on the table as a bond he literally could not flee, signifying a bet he was certain to win.
Two cards of the same rank dealt face-down to a single player as their private 'hole' cards, such as two aces ('pocket rockets').
She'd been dealt pocket kings — the second-best starting hand — and had to decide whether to raise big or disguise their strength.
'Pocket' refers to the hidden hole cards held privately, as if in one's pocket, distinct from the shared community cards on the board.
A hand containing two cards of one rank and two of another, plus a fifth card — for example two jacks and two fives.
Two pair looks strong to a beginner but is a classic trap hand; he overcommitted with jacks and fives and ran straight into a set.
A plainly descriptive term as old as the ranked-hand system of poker itself, which crystallized in mid-19th-century America.
A side card that doesn't form part of a pair or combination but decides the winner when two players hold otherwise equal hands.
They both had a pair of aces, but his king kicker beat her queen — the side card nobody thinks about until it costs them the pot.
From the sense of a card that 'kicks in' to break a tie. The usage is mid-20th-century American poker slang, now universal in the game.
A hand that isn't yet winning but could become strong if the right card arrives — for example four cards to a flush, 'drawing' to the fifth.
He was on a flush draw with one card to come, doing the math: nine cards could complete it, giving him roughly a one-in-five shot.
'Draw' comes from the act of drawing additional cards, central to older draw-poker variants; the 'drawing hand' is one still hoping to improve.
Betting every chip you have on the table in a single commitment. You can't be forced out, but you can't win more than your stake either.
With the tournament on the line he pushed all-in, and the whole table leaned forward — one card now decided whether he went home or doubled up.
The phrase is older than poker, meaning fully committed with nothing held back. Its poker use spread through 20th-century cardrooms and became iconic with televised tournaments.
Checking (passing the action) to feign weakness, then raising after an opponent bets — a trap to extract more chips or win the pot.
She check-raised the river, and you could see her opponent's confidence drain; the check had invited exactly the bet she wanted to punish.
A descriptive compound of the two actions it chains. Once considered unsporting and even banned in some genteel home games, it's now a fundamental tactic.
The ratio of the current pot size to the cost of a call, used to decide whether a draw is mathematically worth chasing.
The pot offered 4-to-1 and his draw would hit about 1-in-5 of the time — the pot odds said call, even though his gut said fold.
A 20th-century formalization as poker strategy grew more mathematical, popularized by theorists like David Sklansky who put numbers to the game's instincts.
Betting or raising with a weak hand to make opponents fold better ones. A pure bluff wins only if everyone folds; a semi-bluff also has cards that can improve.
Daniel Negreanu's real genius isn't bluffing — it's sniffing out others' bluffs, making 'hero calls' that look impossible until the cards turn over.
From Dutch 'bluffen' (to boast or deceive), entering English in the early 1800s as poker spread along the Mississippi. Poker is widely credited with the word's modern meaning.
A 'continuation bet': betting on the next street after you raised before the cards came, continuing the story that you have a strong hand.
He raised before the flop, missed it completely, but fired a c-bet anyway — most of the time, the story alone was enough to take the pot.
A modern term from the online-poker era of the 2000s, when systematic, aggressive post-flop betting was codified and named.
Entering a pot by just calling the minimum rather than raising — often seen as passive or weak play.
He limped in with a marginal hand, and the sharks at the table smelled it instantly; limping told them exactly how little he held.
The image is of slipping into the pot 'limply,' without the strength of a raise. It's 20th-century cardroom slang, now slightly pejorative among serious players.
The third bet in a sequence — a re-raise of an initial raise. A key aggressive move that applies pressure before the flop.
She 3-bet from the blinds, flipping the script; the original raiser now had to defend out of position against a hand he couldn't read.
Bet-counting terminology ('2-bet', '3-bet', '4-bet') emerged from online poker in the 2000s, importing the precise language of limit poker into the no-limit boom.
Deliberately playing a strong hand weakly — checking or just calling — to disguise its strength and lure opponents into betting.
He flopped a full house and slow-played it, checking meekly until the river, by which point his opponent had bet himself broke.
A descriptive term for 'playing slowly,' the opposite of fast, aggressive betting. It's the strategic mirror image of the bluff: hiding strength rather than faking it.
A bet made with a strong hand to get called by weaker ones — betting 'for value' rather than to bluff opponents out.
He had the best hand and needed to get paid, so he sized his value bet just small enough to look temptingly callable.
A modern strategic term distinguishing bets made to profit from a winning hand ('value') from bets made to make better hands fold ('bluffs').
The marker showing who is the nominal dealer, and the most powerful seat — that player acts last after the flop, with the most information.
On the button with position locked up, she could watch everyone else act first and pounce, controlling the size of every pot.
Named for the literal button (or 'buck') passed around the table to mark the dealer — the origin, too, of 'the buck stops here.'
Forced bets posted by the two players left of the button before any cards are seen — the small blind and big blind — that seed the pot.
He was sick of the blinds eating his stack and decided to defend his big blind aggressively for once, refusing to be bullied.
Called 'blind' because the bets are made before seeing any cards — committing money 'blindly.' Blinds replaced older 'ante' structures to force action.
The seat immediately to the right of the button — the second-best position, often used to steal the blinds before the button can act.
From the cutoff he raised to steal, betting that the button and blinds would all fold and hand him the pot uncontested.
Named because a raise from this seat can 'cut off' the button player from entering the pot cheaply, seizing late position from them.
The seat immediately left of the big blind — the first to act before the flop, and the worst position, requiring the strongest hands.
Under the gun with a marginal hand, he folded without a second thought; acting first against eight players is no place for hope.
A 19th-century American idiom meaning 'under pressure,' borrowed into poker for the player forced to act first with no information.
Acting after your opponent on every betting round, giving you the advantage of seeing what they do before you decide.
Being in position let her control the hand entirely — every time he checked, she could bet; every time he bet, she already knew.
A straightforward strategic term; 'position' as the key concept of who acts when became central as poker theory matured in the late 20th century.
An unconscious physical or behavioral cue that reveals information about a player's hand — a trembling hand, a glance, a change in breathing.
The classic tell: weak when strong, strong when weak. He stared down his opponent, who suddenly couldn't stop talking — and folded the winner.
From 'to tell,' meaning to reveal. The poker sense was popularized in the 20th century and cemented by films and Mike Caro's famous 'Book of Tells.'
The full set of hands a player could plausibly hold in a given situation, rather than guessing one specific hand. Thinking in ranges is core to modern strategy.
Instead of asking 'does he have aces?' she thought in ranges — the whole spread of hands he'd play this way — and bet against all of them at once.
A modern, mathematically-minded concept that reshaped expert play in the 2000s, shifting poker thinking from single-hand reads to probability distributions.
Your share of the pot based on your hand's statistical chance of winning — if you'd win 60% of the time, you have 60% equity.
He had the worse hand but more equity to improve, so getting his chips in wasn't a mistake — the math was on his side even when he lost.
Borrowed from finance, where 'equity' means a stake or share. Poker adopted it to express a player's mathematical claim on the pot.
'Game Theory Optimal' play — a mathematically balanced strategy that can't be exploited, even if opponents know exactly what you're doing.
The young pros studied GTO solvers for hours, learning to balance their bluffs and value bets so precisely that no opponent could ever read them.
Rooted in the game theory of John von Neumann and John Nash, GTO entered poker vocabulary in the 2010s with computer solvers that approximated unexploitable play.
The total money a player has set aside specifically for poker. Sound 'bankroll management' means risking only a small fraction in any one game.
He had the skill but blew through three bankrolls before learning the hardest lesson: you can play perfectly and still go broke if you bet too big.
A 19th-century American term for a roll of banknotes; in poker it came to mean the dedicated fund that keeps a player in action through inevitable swings.
Calling a big bet with a relatively weak hand because you've read your opponent as bluffing — a risky, glory-or-disaster move.
Everyone at the table would have folded, but she made the hero call with bottom pair, having decided his story simply didn't add up.
Modern poker slang; the 'hero' is the protagonist making a brave, against-the-odds call, a usage popularized by online forums and televised play.
Losing a hand you were heavily favored to win because your opponent hit an unlikely card. The source of poker's greatest agony.
He had aces against tens, all the money in, and watched a third ten land on the river — the textbook bad beat that haunts players for years.
A 20th-century gambling term; 'beat' for a defeat is old, and 'bad' captures the unfairness of losing as a big favorite. Endless 'bad beat stories' are a poker cliché.
A weak, inexperienced, or losing player — the one the stronger players hope to find at the table. 'If you can't spot the fish, you are the fish.'
The regulars circled the loud tourist dropping chips on every hand; in poker terms, the fish had just sat down at the wrong table.
Gambling slang likening an easy mark to a fish to be caught. The companion term 'shark' completes the predator-and-prey imagery of the cardroom.
An emotional state of frustrated, reckless play, usually after a bad beat — chasing losses and abandoning strategy. 'On tilt.'
After three brutal beats he was visibly on tilt, shoving all-in on hopeless hands, donating his stack to the very players who'd cracked him.
Borrowed from pinball, where nudging the machine too hard triggers 'TILT' and the game locks up — a perfect metaphor for a player whose judgment has gone haywire.
A skilled, often deceptively unassuming player who preys on weaker opponents. The opposite of a fish.
He played the clueless amateur for an hour, then revealed himself as a shark, having quietly mapped every player's tells while pretending to fumble.
Gambling slang since at least the 1800s, from 'sharper' (a cheat or schemer). The fish-and-shark pairing makes the cardroom food chain vivid.
A derogatory term for a bad player ('donkey'), and 'donk bet' for the unconventional move of betting into the previous round's aggressor.
He made a donk bet into the raiser, and the table couldn't tell if it was a genius trap or, more likely, exactly the donkey play it looked like.
Short for 'donkey,' implying a stubborn, foolish player. It rose with online poker in the 2000s, both as an insult and, in 'donk bet,' a named tactic.
The fifth and final community card. By extension, 'to be rivered' is to lose when that last card gives an opponent the winning hand.
He was ahead the whole way and got rivered — that final card pairing the board and snatching the pot from his hands at the last instant.
Possibly from riverboat poker, where a cheat caught dealing from the bottom could be thrown in the river — or simply the last card 'flowing' out. The origin is debated.
A hand where two players both make very strong holdings and one inevitably loses a huge pot — bad luck rather than bad play.
Full house against four of a kind: a pure cooler. Nobody could have folded, and he shrugged it off — there's no lesson in a hand you couldn't escape.
Modern poker slang, from the idea of a situation that 'cools off' a hot run of cards. It absolves the loser: a cooler is misfortune, not a mistake.
